**ABSTRACT**[ 28 ]. This automation aims to alleviate the charge associated with manual test writing and to enhance overall test effectiveness andWritingunit tests is a time-consuming and labor-intensive develop- efficiency.ment practice. Consequently, various techniques for automatically Recent research by Schäfer et.aland El Haji et al[7] high-generating unit tests have been studied. Among them, the use of lights the substantial capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs)Large Language Models (LLMs) has recently emerged as a popular in generating test code. These studies commonly employ a strategyapproachfor automatically generating tests from natural language 12where LLMs,suchas ChatGPT, GitHubCopilot , andAmazondescriptions. Although many recent studies are dedicated to mea- 3CodeWhisperer, are pre-trained using natural language prompts.suring the ability of LLMs to write valid unit tests, few evaluate the Such prompts may drive test code generation that aligns with the an-quality of these generated tests. In this context, this study aims to ticipatedoutcomes. This method capitalizes on the models’ human-measure the quality of the test codes generated by GitHub Copilot like textcomprehensionandgenerationabilities,therebyfacili-in Python by detecting test smells in the test cases generated.To tating the creation of customizedtest code for distinctuse casesdo this, we used approaches to generating unit tests by LLMs that [ 35 , 37]. LLMs’ depth of knowledge and contextual understandinghave already been applied in the literature and collected a sample enables them to transcend traditional testing limitations, probingof 194 unittestcasesin 30 Pythontest files.We thenmeasured diversescenarios and identifying potential issues that conventionalthem using tools specialized in detecting test smells in Python. Fi- methods might overlook.nally,we conducted an evaluation of these test cases with software While LLMs are increasingly indicated as the future of code gen-developers and software quality assuranceprofessionals.Our re- eration, a growing body of research is focused on evaluatingthesults indicated that 47.4% of the tests generated by Copilot had at quality of the code they produce. Hanssonand Ellréus[13] haveleast one test smell detected, with a lack of documentationin the noted that code generated by models such as ChatGPT and GitHubassertionsbeing the most common quality problem. These findings Copilot sometimes violates established quality guidelines. Further-indicatethat although GitHub Copilot can generate valid unit tests, more, in production code crafted by various LLMs, Yetiştiren.et alquality violations are still frequently found in these codes. [36] has conducted a thorough analysis of ’code smells’—indicators of deeper issues affecting maintainability and reliability. The find-**CCS CONCEPTS** ings suggest that, despite the correctness of most generated code, • Software and its engineering→testingand de- there aresignificantconcernsregardingitsmaintainabilityand **bugging ; • Artificial Intelligence → Large Language Models**; trustworthiness • General and reference→Empirical studies. In the domain of test code generation, although numerous stud- ies have addressed the generation capabilities of LLMs, a significant **KEYWORDS** researchgap remains concerning the quality of test code produced TestSmells, Large Language Models, Python Test Codeby these models [,29,30]. Traditional testing methodologies often encounter issues with test cases, commonly knowntestas smells, **1 INTRODUCTION**which maysignaldeficienciesin testdesignor implementation. These shortcomingscan lead to decreased efficiency in detectingUnit testingisafoundationofsoftwarequalityassurance,pro- failures or validating software behavior31[]. Test smells can mani-viding a mechanism for evaluating the functionality of individual fest in various forms, such as poorly structured test code or overlyunits of source code in isolation11[ Despite its critical role, the complex logic, which complicates both understanding and mainte-creation of unit test code is often overlookedby developers,pre- nance of the test code [16].dominantly due to its complexity and the time-consuming nature of the task [, 5, 25]. The manualgenerationof tests is not only labor-intensive but also costly17[ ]. Consequently, there is a grow-1[https://chat.openai.com/](https://chat.openai.com/) 2ing emphasis within the development community on developing[https://github.com/features/copilot](https://github.com/features/copilot) 3tools and methodologies that automate the generation of unit tests[https://aws.amazon.com/pt/codewhisperer/](https://aws.amazon.com/pt/codewhisperer/)

In this context, the main objective of this research project is toLLMs have assumed a significant role in test generation driven by conduct an empirical study on the quality of test code generatedNatural Language Processing (NLP)37 by LLMs, with a specific focus on GitHub Copilot for Python. Thecode generation aim is to investigatewhetherthetestsgeneratedby thesetoolsthe time and costs associated can be trusted and whether the code produced is well structured.By training To achieve this, unit test codes were generated from open sourceCodex[4] can Python projects. After generating the test code, a detailed analysisrepair bugs. This functionality has been extended in applications was carried out to assess its quality. This process involved the uselike GitHub of specialized tools to identifytest smells. Finally, we carried out ancapability in test code generation7 evaluationof the test cases generated by Copilot by software quality ments, there remains a gap in the and development professionals. This study provides findings on thegenerated by these models. reliability of test codes generated by GitHub Copilot, focusing on the frequency of detection of test smells, the most common types**3 STUDY** of testsmellsandidentifiablepatterns,aswellaspractitioners’ **3.1 Goals** perceptions of the quality of these codes. This study aims to investigate potential irregularities and quality violations in test code generated**2 BACKGROUND** chosen for this investigation due to its extensive support by Codex **2.1 Unit**Testing & Test Smells[ 4 ]. GitHub Unit testing is a technique designed to detect defects and validateits broad integration with Integrated Development Environments the functionalityof the smallesttestableparts of software,such(IDEs) and its capability to access and interpret code files as modules, objects, and classes, which can be examined in isola- achieve these goals, three research questions were formulated: 4tion [12]. Automatedtest generation facilitates the production and execution of numerous inputs that thoroughly test software units34 ].[ Tradi-**3.2 Study** tional approaches to test generation have included model-driven,Wesplit this study into four phases. First, we selected open-source requirement-based,static analysis, and research-driven techniquesprojects. Next, we employed GitHub Copilot to generate test code [19, 21].for these projects. by GitHub Copilot. Python was

by GitHub Copilot?This question seeks to identify commontypesof test smellsand to discernany

---

<!-- Página 3 -->

Detecting An

**Figure 1: Methodological procedures**

most popular open-source projects and using small and medium- sized projects allows for more complete LLM training. Furthermore, we chose projects that already had unit tests to facilitate the identifi- cation of the most testable and critical parts of the production code. However, existing unit tests were disregarded for Codex training, and only production codes were used. This approach aims to ensure a more complete, accurate and targeted analysis of the test codes generated by GitHub Copilot withoutinfluencefrom those tests alreadypresent in the project. The training was carried out individ- ually for each project, aiming to minimize information conflicts and the occurrence of hallucinations20[ ] during test generation. Table 1 contains all the projects used in this study and their respective information.

**Table 1: List of open-source projects used in this study**

**Project****Domain****#****#****#** python-lottie clickCLI1015767508 Domain pyflunt2969 Design brutils-pythonPython284925140

3.2.2 TestCode Generation.Once the open-sourceprojectshad been selected,the productioncodes were chosen as the basis for generatingthe unit tests. To do this, Python files containing classes, methods or functions with testable returns were considered. Thus, for each production code file𝐶, a test Python file𝑇was generated.𝑖𝑖 For each pair𝐶(,𝑇), GitHub Copilot generated the test case𝐶𝑇,𝑖𝑖𝑖,𝑗 with𝑗 being able to vary from 5 to 20 test cases per file. The libraries 1112used to generate the test codes were pytestand unittest. Togenerate each test file𝑇 , we submitted a series of prompts so𝑖 that Copilot could generate the tests according to the specifications. Each prompt should contain informationabout (i) which classes, methods, or functions would be tested, (ii) the types of scenarios to consider(successful,alternative,and exception),(iii) possible restrictionsto be taken into account, such as the maximum number Table 2 lists the test smells that were addressed in this study and 11[https://docs.pytest.org/en/8.2.x/](https://docs.pytest.org/en/8.2.x/) 12[https://docs.python.org/3/library/unittest.html](https://docs.python.org/3/library/unittest.html)

of test cases, disregarded, and (iv) the test library that would be used. Figure 2 shows a generic example of the prompt created:

**Figure 2: General prompt used to generate the test files**

After submitting the command prompt to Copilot, the unit tests were returned ecution. A sample of 30 pairs test cases. All the test cases were executed, and only the valid ones were considered for this study. We which Python After filtering, the sample was reduced to 194 valid test cases.

3.2.3 Test erated and filtered, a series of Python-specific tools were selected to detect test smells in the code. Three different tools were used for this analysis: Pynose [ them has its own techniques categorizing test smells, providing a broader view of the quality of the test code generated by GitHub Copilot. We call test codes those Python𝑇 tified in the corresponding test cases, which we call test cases

the tools that identified them. The list containing all the test cases and detections is available at the end of this paper.

and stored in each project’s directory

interpreted

smells detection tools.

<!-- Página 5 -->

Detecting AnSBES’24,

the conceptsproposedby van Deursenet. [32]al, indicateviola- **Figure 3: % Test smells detected by professionals for each set**tions in the tests generated by Copilot, including the excessive use **of test cases**of undocumentedassertions(Assertion Roulette), the presence of numeric literal values(Magic Number Test)and the complexity of conditionallogic(ConditionalTest Logic). In addition, the tools also identified test cases without assertions(Unknown Test), duplicate test cases(DuplicateAssert) and testthat make excessive calls to methods in production code(Eager Test). Answerto RQ:The tests generated by GitHub Copilot revealed2 a diversity of detected test smells. The most common test smell was AssertionRoulette, observed in 42% of the tests, suggesting an exces- sive use of undocumented assertions. In addition, other test smells were also identified,such asMagic Number Test, ConditionalTest Logic, Unknown Test, Duplicate Assert, Eager Test and Test Maverick, although less frequently. These results highlight specific areas in which the test codes generated by Copilot can be improved.

**4.3 RQ****: Practitioners’ Perceptions**3 ToanswerRQ, we performed a qualitative and quantitative anal-3 ysis of the evaluationsprovided by industryprofessionalsin the online questionnaire. In the quantitative analysis, we counted how many times each test smell was identified by professionals in each set of test cases. Table 6 presents the types of test smells included made it difficult for some to distinguish between two or more tests.in the questionnaire.The columnsreferringto denote the six𝑖 Severalother problems related to readability were also identified, ac-sets of test cases evaluated and the absolute frequency with which companiedby suggestions for improvement. Some reports on theseprofessionals in their evaluations identified each test smell. Figure problems included lack of variables, repetition of code structure in3 presentsa visualsampleof the percentageof identificationof more than one test, lack of context in the naming of tests, objectseach test smell in the evaluation. and variables, among others. In addition, professionals pointed out that the tests generated by Copilot did not take advantage of most**Table 6: Test Smells detected by practitioners** of the resources offered by thepytest and unittest libraries, which may havecontributedtotheproblemofcodereadability.Some**Test**𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆123456 Assertionreports on these problems were left on the form: Conditional6111731 MagicP9:“It Duplicate102530variables, Unknow argumentEager981171210

### investigated the

### presence

### of

### code

Korea</country>,(ISSTA. Association

### smells in the codes generated by Amazon’s CodeWhisperer, Copilot

ery, [4]Mark

### and ChatGPT. They found that certain code smells tend to recur in

Oliveira

### the generated code and that the LLMs themselves have the ability to

Greg Khlaaf,

### fix these maintenance problems. This pattern of repeating problems

Mikhail

### that impact code maintainability

### was also identified

### by Hansson

Winter,

### and Ellréus

### [13] , but the authors

### emphasized

### that ChatGPT

### and

Fotios Alex

### GitHub Copilot

### have

### proven

### to

### be

### more

### effective

### in

### generating

Shantanu

### quality

### code. Thus, although there have been several evaluations of

Joshua

### the quality of code produced by LLMs, these analyses have focused

Miles
